question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Massachusetts, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Massachusetts Public Records Law (M.G.L. c. 66, § 10), agencies (known as Records Access Officers or RAOs) generally must respond to public records requests within 10 business days of receipt. This applies to records requests directed at MassHealth (Massachusetts Medicaid) as it does to other state agencies. The initial response must either provide the records, identify any records being withheld with reasons, or provide a good-faith estimate of the time and fees required. Extensions may be granted by the Supervisor of Records for up to 20 additional business days (or longer in limited circumstances).

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Massachusetts, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Massachusetts Public Records Law (M.G.L. c. 66, § 10), agencies (known as Records Access Officers) must respond to public records requests within 10 business days of receipt. This applies to requests for Medicaid (MassHealth) records as well. The initial response must either provide the records, indicate when they will be provided, or explain any applicable exemptions. Agencies may request an extension from the Supervisor of Records for more complex requests (up to 25 business days for municipalities and an extended period for state agencies, subject to approval).
source: https://www.mass.gov/handbook/a-guide-to-the-massachusetts-public-records-law (Massachusetts Secretary of the Commonwealth, Public Records Division); M.G.L. c. 66, § 10

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Massachusetts, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Massachusetts Public Records Law (M.G.L. c. 66, § 10), agencies (records access officers) generally must respond to public records requests within 10 business days of receipt. This applies to records requests made to MassHealth (the Massachusetts Medicaid agency) as well as other state agencies. The initial response must either provide the records, identify them and give a reasonable timeline for production, or explain any denial. If more time is needed, an agency can request an extension from the Supervisor of Records, but any extension generally cannot exceed 25 business days from the initial receipt for state agencies without further approval.
source: https://www.mass.gov/public-records-law (Massachusetts Public Records Law, M.G.L. c. 66, § 10); 950 CMR 32.00

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Rhode Island, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Rhode Island Access to Public Records Act (APRA), public agencies generally have 10 business days to respond to a public records request. question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In California, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the California Public Records Act (Government Code section 7922.535, formerly section 6253), agencies must determine whether a request seeks disclosable public records and notify the requester of that determination within 10 calendar days of receiving the request. In unusual circumstances, this deadline may be extended by up to 14 additional calendar days with written notice to the requester. Note that the initial 10-day response is a determination of whether records will be produced, not necessarily the production of the records themselves; records must then be produced promptly. This applies to records held by state agencies, including the Department of Health Care Services (which administers Medi-Cal, California's Medicaid program).

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Texas, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Texas Public Information Act (Chapter 552 of the Texas Government Code), governmental bodies generally must 'promptly' produce public information in response to a request. If the agency believes the requested information may be excepted from disclosure, it must request an Attorney General decision within 10 business days of receiving the request. There is no special, separate deadline for Medicaid-related records; they are subject to the same timeframes, though Medicaid records may be withheld or redacted under confidentiality provisions (such as HIPAA and federal Medicaid confidentiality rules).
source: Texas Government Code Chapter 552 (Public Information Act), https://statutes.capitol.texas.gov/Docs/GV/htm/GV.552.htm; Texas Attorney General Public Information Handbook, https://www.texasattorneygeneral.gov/open-government/members-public/public-information-act

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Texas, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?

If the agency believes the requested information may be withheld or excepted from disclosure, it must request an attorney general's decision within 10 business days of receiving the request. There is no fixed statutory deadline for producing records, but production must be prompt; if information cannot be produced within 10 business days, the agency must certify that fact in writing and set a reasonable date and time for production.
source: Texas Government Code Chapter 552 (Public Information Act), https://statutes.capitol.texas.gov/Docs/GV/htm/GV.552.htm; Texas Attorney General Public Information Handbook, https://www.texasattorneygeneral.gov/open-government/governmental-bodies/public-information-act
